author: @verdigrissirgidrev4152
text:
full recipe, hope I got it right:
15ml ammonia 9.5%
200ml water, dist.
1/4 tsp sulfuric acid 95% (toilet cleaner)
==> ammonium sulfate
7.5g NaOH/caustic soda
24ml phosphoric acid 20% (=6g, limescale remover) 
top off w/ water to 200ml
250ml citric acid monohydrate 15% (rust remover, instead of oxalic acid)
top of again, to 1L.
hope you don't mind me listing it here in text form.

author: @BuyMyDreamHotel
text:
Hi Robert

I wondered if you would be good enough to list the "pure" chemicals weights?

Since I played around with the "oxalic" version I have all the chemicals required in pure form so I do not need to mix the various cleaning agents.

I followed the video calculating/verifying effective pure weights but I was left uncertain regards the citric as I was not sure how much citric acid would be required to replace the oxalic acid.

I was also interested to know how you arrived at that amount of citric acid - trial and error or other?

Thanks Jon

replies.author: @BuyMyDreamHotel
replies.text:
The citric acid is 15% by weight - 250ml gives 37.5g vs 32g oxalic.

Molar weight citric monohydrate is 210g/mol and oxalic acid is 90g/mol so it cant be a straight forward mole for mole swap? 

Would be grateful if you could confirm 37.5g citric acid monohydrate per litre or indicate what it should be as appropriate, I would like to try this one out with a straight plating set up rather than brush.

Many thanks 
Jon

author: @allyoops7020
text: Hi have a Theory!  Gold Plating Solution.  How to produce this is a well kept secret, or is it i can find it?   There are a few way to dissolve gold in acid, one such way is to combine Sulfuric acid with Hydrochloric acid to produce this produce Aqua Regia.   This will dissolve gold.  But now left with a acid soup that cant be handled easily.  Thought came across me, what if after this process you neutralise the acids by mixing with sodium bicarbonate??  You should end with a PH level 6-9.  Your thoughts.  I know you have moved on to greater things. (:  Ally

replies.author: @veraprokic4185
replies.text: +Ally oops    mistake! Aqua Regia is- hydrochloric acid +nitric acid, known to dissolve gold.

author: @bornhabercycle3530
text: Has anyone managed using this or the original oxalic acid formula - to get nickel to plate onto steel. I have tried lots of combinations, with some minor success but it never seems to be reliable. The steel has been properly cleaned a degrease etc. Thanks.

replies.author: @hamishmiddleton3478
replies.text: Assuming you are talking about STAINLESS steel, I use a very simple (and very effective) solution of dilute hydrochloric acid which I brush onto the steel at around 5V. I then thoroughly rinse the acid from the steel and proceed to the metal plating stage. I normally pre-plate the steel with copper first before plating with any other metal. The reason for this is it is actually the pre-plated layer of copper that helps the nickel adhere to the base metal.

author: @icantgetpregnant
text:
Hi Robert fantastic set of videos and info, Can you pleas answer me this question? Can you please give a list of metals that can be plated with your solution, or a list that cannot be plated.

Big thanks Clive

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Glad you liked them and hope they are useful. The plating solution is universal. What you have to do is change the metal on the plating brush - so instead of using a copper plate you would need to fix on a gold plate - i suggest an old ring - I have one somewhere ina drawer I may yet pull out to plate with.
